canto_title: The Third Circle: The Wrathful and the Rain of Fire

scenes.summary: Dante and Virgil move from the second circle to the third, where the horrors of justice are revealed.
The poets arrive at a barren wasteland characterized by a thick, arid sand.
Dante observes a vast, miserable crowd of naked souls undergoing various forms of torment.
The souls are described in different positions—lying down, sitting, or walking—yet all are vocal in their grief.
Great flakes of fire fall upon the sand like snow, heating the earth to intensify the suffering.
Dante asks Virgil about the massive figure who appears indifferent to the fire and the surrounding torment.
The soul Capaneus replies, declaring that his rage remains as strong in death as it was in life.
Virgil condemns Capaneus's pride and warns Dante to keep his feet on the edges of the path.
The poets reach a small, blood-red stream that flows through the wasteland.
Virgil asks Dante if he noticed anything notable about the recent horrors of the third circle.
Virgil describes a ruined landscape in Crete involving the myth of Rhea and the mountain Ida.
Virgil describes a massive, multi-metal figure standing upright in the mountains.
The tears from the colossus's fissures flow down to form the various rivers of Hell.
Dante wonders why they can see this place if it is so far from the world, and Virgil explains the circular nature of Hell.
Dante asks about the Phlegethon and Lethe, and Virgil explains the function of the red boiling water and the river of forgetfulness.
Virgil instructs Dante to follow him along the safe, unburned edges of the path, away from the woods.
